choices.text: Aircraft identification, type, ETA, and type of approach or SVFR/contact approach details.
Aircraft identification and ETA only; other information is obtained from the flight plan.
Only the type of approach procedure; other information is forwarded separately.
Aircraft identification, ETA, and parking gate assignment.

operations.problem: The explanation uses paragraph-location scaffolding: 'Paragraph 4-7-13 requires coordination...'
operations.source_basis: When a change is made from one ILS to another at airports equipped with multiple systems which are not used simultaneously, coordinate with the facilities which use the fixes formed by reference to these NAVAIDs.

match.question_text: When changing from one ILS to another at an airport equipped with multiple ILS systems that operate one at a time, what coordination is required?

replacements.question_text: When changing from one ILS to another at an airport equipped with multiple ILS systems that operate one at a time, what coordination is required?
replacements.explanation: When switching from one ILS to another, coordinate with all facilities that use fixes defined by reference to those ILS NAVAIDs. A change in the ILS in use affects any fix defined relative to that NAVAID, and all facilities using those fixes must be notified of the change.

choices.text: Coordinate with the facilities that use fixes formed by reference to the ILS NAVAIDs.
Coordinate with the airport operator and the ILS maintenance technician.
No coordination is required; the change is handled through NOTAMs.
Coordinate only with the TRACON, which handles all ILS changes.

operations.problem: The explanation uses document-structure scaffolding: 'Both subparagraphs (a) and (b) create duties...'
operations.source_basis: Take the following action when exercising control of aircraft landing at an airport located in an adjacent center's control area near the common boundary: a. Coordinate with the receiving facility for a lower altitude and issue a clearance to the aircraft as appropriate. b. Initiate this action at a distance sufficient from destination to allow for normal descent and speed reduction.

match.question_text: An aircraft is landing at an airport located in an adjacent center's control area near the common boundary. The controller at the first center needs to issue a lower altitude. What two actions must the controller take when exercising control of aircraft landing at an airport in an adjacent center's area near the boundary?

replacements.question_text: An aircraft is landing at an airport located in an adjacent center's control area near the common boundary. The controller at the first center needs to issue a lower altitude. What two actions must the controller take when exercising control of aircraft landing at an airport in an adjacent center's area near the boundary?
replacements.explanation: When an aircraft is landing at an airport in an adjacent center's control area near the common boundary, the controller must coordinate with the receiving facility for the lower altitude and issue the clearance as appropriate, and must initiate this action at a sufficient distance from the destination to allow for normal descent and speed reduction.

choices.text: Issue the descent clearance immediately, then coordinate with the receiving facility after the aircraft has started descent.
Transfer communications to the adjacent center and let that facility issue the descent clearance.
Coordinate with the receiving facility for the lower altitude and initiate the action at a sufficient distance for normal descent and speed reduction.
Coordinate with the receiving facility only; the adjacent center issues the actual descent clearance.

operations.problem: The explanation invents an unsupported rationale: 'These exceptions balance the SFA safety benefit against operational necessity.' The source lists the five conditions but does not characterize them as balancing safety against operational necessity.
operations.source_basis: do not require a radio frequency change after the aircraft begins approach or after initial contact during an en route descent until a landing or low approach has been completed except under the following conditions: a. During daylight hours while the aircraft is in VFR conditions. b. On pilot request. c. When pilot cancels IFR flight plan. d. In an emergency situation. e. When aircraft is cleared for visual approach.

match.question_text: What are the five conditions under which a frequency change is permitted during a Single Frequency Approach?

replacements.question_text: What are the five conditions under which a frequency change is permitted during a Single Frequency Approach?
replacements.explanation: A frequency change is permitted during a Single Frequency Approach only under five conditions: during daylight hours while the aircraft is in VFR conditions, on pilot request, when the pilot cancels the IFR flight plan, in an emergency situation, or when the aircraft is cleared for a visual approach.

choices.text: Daylight VFR, pilot request, IFR cancellation, emergency, or visual approach clearance.
Pilot request, radar contact, VMC, DME available, and landing assured.
Emergency only, with no other exceptions permitted.
Night VFR, pilot request, IFR cancellation, equipment failure, or visual approach clearance.

operations.problem: The explanation uses document-structure scaffolding: 'The paragraph has a two-part test for nonradar use...'
operations.source_basis: An en route descent may be used in a nonradar environment; however, radar capability should exist which will permit the aircraft to be vectored to the final approach course of a published high altitude instrument approach procedure or PAR/ASR approach. Do not use this procedure if other than normal vectoring delays are anticipated.

match.question_text: A military turbojet aircraft is in a nonradar environment, and the controller plans to use the en route descent procedure. Radar capability exists to vector the aircraft to the final approach course of a published high altitude instrument approach. However, the controller anticipates a 10-minute vectoring delay due to traffic volume. May the en route descent procedure be used?

replacements.question_text: A military turbojet aircraft is in a nonradar environment, and the controller plans to use the en route descent procedure. Radar capability exists to vector the aircraft to the final approach course of a published high altitude instrument approach. However, the controller anticipates a 10-minute vectoring delay due to traffic volume. May the en route descent procedure be used?
replacements.explanation: For nonradar use of the en route descent procedure, radar capability must exist to vector the aircraft to the final approach course of a published high altitude instrument approach or PAR/ASR approach, and the controller must not anticipate other-than-normal vectoring delays. Both conditions must be satisfied.

choices.text: Yes. The delay only prohibits use in a radar environment; in a nonradar environment, the delay restriction does not apply.
Yes. Radar capability exists to vector the aircraft, which satisfies the only condition for using the procedure.
No. The 10-minute vectoring delay is 'other than normal,' which specifically prohibits use of this procedure.
No. The en route descent procedure can never be used in a nonradar environment.

operations.problem: The explanation uses paragraph-location scaffolding: 'The note to 4-7-5 states...'
operations.source_basis: It is the responsibility of the pilot to request a high altitude approach if he/she does not want normal arrival handling.

match.question_text: Who is responsible for requesting a high altitude approach when normal arrival handling is unsuitable for a military turbojet aircraft?

replacements.question_text: Who is responsible for requesting a high altitude approach when normal arrival handling is unsuitable for a military turbojet aircraft?
replacements.explanation: The pilot is responsible for requesting a high altitude approach when normal arrival handling is not desired. The controller provides normal arrival procedures by default; the pilot must affirmatively request the high altitude alternative.

choices.text: The pilot, who must request a high altitude approach.
The controller, who must offer it to all military aircraft.
The operations officer, who must specify it in the flight plan remarks.
The receiving facility, which determines the approach type.

operations.problem: The explanation uses paragraph-location scaffolding: 'Paragraph 4-7-6b4 states the clearance limit...' and 'Similarly, b5 allows omission...'

Time, fix, or altitude when control responsibility is transferred to the approach control facility. This information may be omitted when provided for in a letter of agreement.

match.question_text: When forwarding arrival information to approach control facilities before transfer of control jurisdiction, what information may be omitted when provided for in a letter of agreement?

replacements.question_text: When forwarding arrival information to approach control facilities before transfer of control jurisdiction, what information may be omitted when provided for in a letter of agreement?
replacements.explanation: The clearance limit and the time, fix, or altitude at which control responsibility transfers may each be omitted from arrival information forwarded to approach control facilities when provided for in a letter of agreement. choices.text: The clearance limit and the time, fix, or altitude of control transfer.
The aircraft identification and type.
The ETA and actual time over the clearance limit.
The aircraft equipment suffix and proposed altitude.

operations.problem: The explanation uses paragraph-location scaffolding: 'Paragraph 4-7-7 triggers the weather transmission requirement...'
operations.source_basis: When an available official weather report indicates weather conditions are below a 1,000-foot (USAF: 1,500-foot) ceiling or below the highest circling minimum, whichever is higher, or less than three-miles visibility for the airport concerned, transmit the weather report and changes classified as special weather observations to an arriving aircraft prior to or as part of the approach clearance.

match.question_text: What weather conditions trigger the requirement to transmit weather reports to arriving aircraft prior to or as part of the approach clearance?

replacements.question_text: What weather conditions trigger the requirement to transmit weather reports to arriving aircraft prior to or as part of the approach clearance?
replacements.explanation: The weather transmission requirement is triggered when the ceiling is below 1,000 feet (USAF: 1,500 feet) or below the highest circling minimum — whichever is higher — or when visibility is less than 3 miles. Either the ceiling condition or the visibility condition alone is sufficient to trigger the requirement.

choices.text: Ceiling below 1,000 feet (or highest circling minimum) or visibility less than 3 miles.
Ceiling below 500 feet or visibility less than 1 mile.
Any IFR conditions at the destination airport.
Whenever the destination ATIS is out of service.

operations.problem: The explanation uses document-structure scaffolding: 'The note makes clear that the pilot determines weather adequacy...'
operations.source_basis: If an arriving aircraft reports weather conditions are below his/her landing minima: a. Issue appropriate instructions to the aircraft to hold or proceed to another airport. b. Adjust, as necessary, the position in the landing sequence of any other aircraft desiring to make approaches and issue approach clearances accordingly. NOTE: Determination that existing weather/visibility is adequate for approach/landing is the responsibility of the pilot/aircraft operator.

match.question_text: An arriving aircraft reports that weather conditions at the destination are below the pilot's landing minima. What are the controller's required actions upon receiving a pilot report of below-minima weather at the destination?

replacements.question_text: An arriving aircraft reports that weather conditions at the destination are below the pilot's landing minima. What are the controller's required actions upon receiving a pilot report of below-minima weather at the destination?

Issue instructions to hold or proceed to another airport, and adjust the landing sequence of other aircraft as necessary.
Declare an emergency on behalf of the aircraft and provide priority handling.
Ask the pilot to attempt the approach anyway because weather observations can be inaccurate.

Distractors (alternate airport suggestion, frequency for the next sector, supervisor's name) are not plausible ATC communications in this operational context. The item is a near-duplicate of ab7d5b2f — both are multiple-choice questions testing the same subparagraph 4-8-11a rule about communicating reason and anticipated delay when denying a practice approach. ab7d5b2f covers this with a richer scenario stem.
operations.source_basis: 4-8-11d4(a): 'Where a facility directive does not require the application of IFR separation services to VFR aircraft practicing instrument approaches, the controller must: (a) Instruct the pilot to maintain VFR and advise the pilot that separation services are not provided.' The note clarifies: 'This advisory is intended to remind the pilot that even though ATC is providing IFR-type instructions, the pilot is responsible for compliance with the CFRs governing VFR flight.'

match.question_text: When a controller cannot approve a practice instrument approach due to workload, what two things must be communicated to the pilot?

replacements.question_text: A VFR aircraft requests a practice instrument approach at a facility whose directive does not require IFR separation services for VFR practice approaches. Which of the following is a required controller action when approving the approach?
replacements.explanation: When a facility directive does not require IFR separation for VFR practice instrument approaches, the controller must instruct the pilot to maintain VFR and advise that separation services are not provided. The controller must also provide traffic information or advise the pilot to contact the appropriate facility. The prescribed phraseology is 'MAINTAIN VFR, PRACTICE APPROACH APPROVED, NO SEPARATION SERVICES PROVIDED.' The pilot remains responsible for compliance with the CFRs governing VFR flight.

choices.text: Instruct the pilot to maintain VFR and advise that separation services are not provided.
Terminate radar service to the aircraft before issuing the practice approach clearance.
Issue the approach clearance and instruct the pilot to monitor the approach frequency for traffic advisories.
Advise the pilot that practice approaches are not authorized without IFR separation services.

operations.problem: The explanation opens with paragraph-location scaffolding ('Paragraph 4-8-7 states the side-step maneuver may be used…'), violating the rule that explanations must state the operating requirement directly. The stem, choices, and correct answer are sound.
operations.source_basis: 4-8-7 body: 'When authorized by an instrument approach procedure, you may clear an aircraft for an approach to one runway and inform the aircraft that landing will be made on a parallel runway.' Note: 'Side-step maneuvers require higher weather minima/MDA. These higher minima/MDA are published on the instrument approach charts.'

match.question_text: When is a controller authorized to clear an aircraft for a side-step maneuver?

replacements.question_text: When is a controller authorized to clear an aircraft for a side-step maneuver?
replacements.explanation: A controller may clear an aircraft for a side-step maneuver only when the published instrument approach procedure authorizes it. The controller cannot independently create a side-step to a parallel runway — the IAP must include the maneuver. Side-step maneuvers require higher weather minima or MDA, which are published on the instrument approach chart.

choices.text: When authorized by a published instrument approach procedure.
Whenever the pilot requests to land on a parallel runway.
When the primary runway is occupied and a parallel runway is available.
At any airport with parallel runways separated by less than 2,500 feet.

operations.problem: This item is a near-duplicate of 5e9e07bb. Both are multiple-choice questions testing the same operational rule from 4-8-8: the condition under which a controller may approve an IFR aircraft's frequency change to the advisory frequency at a non-towered airport. 5e9e07bb is the stronger item — it uses a self-contained scenario stem and tests the prescribed phraseology. 5441dda0 adds no distinct educational coverage; its correct answer ('When the controller no longer requires direct communications') is necessarily understood to answer 5e9e07bb correctly.
operations.source_basis: 4-8-8 body: 'If an IFR aircraft intends to land at an airport not served by a tower or FSS, approve a change to the advisory service frequency when you no longer require direct communications.' Item 5e9e07bb already tests this condition within a richer operational scenario that also covers the prescribed phraseology.

operations.problem: The stem asks 'Under what condition may the controller omit issuing the full description of an alternate missed approach procedure?' but the designated correct answer describes when the controller must issue the full description — the opposite of what was asked. None of the four choices state the actual condition for omission (the alternate procedure is published and in effect via NOTAM). Additionally, the stem embeds the answer to its own operational scenario, making the set-up self-resolving, and the explanation uses paragraph-location scaffolding ('4-8-9c,' '4-8-9b').
operations.source_basis: 4-8-9b: 'If assignment of the alternate missed approach procedure is necessary, the controller must issue the description as published on the appropriate FAA Form 8260 or military form for the IAP.' 4-8-9c: 'If the alternate missed approach procedure is published and in effect via NOTAM, the procedure description need not be issued to the pilot.'

match.question_text: An IFR aircraft on an ILS approach executes a missed approach at the decision altitude. The controller needs the aircraft to follow the published alternate missed approach procedure instead of the primary procedure. The alternate procedure appears on FAA Form 8260 but requires controller issuance of the full description because it is pending NOTAM activation. Under what condition may the controller omit issuing the full description of an alternate missed approach procedure?

replacements.question_text: A controller assigns the published alternate missed approach procedure to an IFR aircraft. The alternate procedure is not activated by NOTAM. What must the controller do regarding the procedure description?
replacements.explanation: When an alternate missed approach procedure is assigned and it is not published and in effect via NOTAM, the controller must issue the full description as published on the appropriate FAA Form 8260. The NOTAM exception relieves the controller of this duty only when the alternate procedure is published and in effect via NOTAM.

choices.text: Issue the full description of the alternate missed approach procedure as published on FAA Form 8260.
Omit the description; the procedure is available on the published approach chart and the pilot is expected to reference it.
Advise the pilot that an alternate missed approach is assigned and that further instructions will follow after the missed approach commences.
Issue only the initial heading and altitude for the alternate procedure; the remainder is available on the approach chart.
